findings.label: Does the deck include a clearly identified Problem slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as PROBLEM
findings.suggestion: You've buried your problem statement inside a "Startup Information" section on slide 2 when it deserves its own dedicated slide early in the deck—ideally right after your cover slide. Pull out that paragraph about manual processes, fragmented brokers, delayed lead responses, and agent burnout, give it visual breathing room with a clear headline like "The Problem: Real Estate's Manual Bottleneck," and lead with one or two concrete numbers (like "agents spend 60% of time on admin tasks" or "average response time to leads is 48+ hours") before diving into the full narrative. This makes the pain point impossible to miss and gives investors a clear reason to care before you show them your solution.

findings.label: Does the deck include a Solution slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as SOLUTION
findings.suggestion: You've got your problem statement and business model laid out clearly, but you're missing a dedicated slide that shows investors exactly how MIRA works from a user's perspective. Create a single "How It Works" slide that walks through one complete transaction in 4-5 visual steps—for example: Customer asks MIRA on WhatsApp → AI qualifies and matches to 3 properties → Customer views AR floor plans and ROI dashboard → MIRA handles docs and mortgage coordination → Deal closes. This gives investors a concrete mental model of your value delivery instead of making them piece it together from your product features list.

findings.label: Does the deck include a Team slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 8, Slide 9, Slide 10

findings.label: Does the deck include a Financials slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as FINANCIALS
findings.suggestion: You have AED 800K in revenue and strong early traction, but investors won't know how sustainable it is without seeing your unit economics—add a one-page financials slide showing your revenue breakdown by stream (what % is commission vs. developer partnerships vs. SaaS), your monthly burn rate, and your CAC-to-LTV ratio for at least one customer segment. For example: "Commission-based revenue: AED 500K (62%), Developer partnerships: AED 200K (25%), SaaS: AED 100K (13%) | Monthly burn: AED 150K | B2C CAC: AED 8K, LTV: AED 45

findings.label: Does the deck include a Traction slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 6

findings.label: Does the deck include a Market slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as MARKET
findings.suggestion: You've got strong traction numbers (AED 50M in transactions, AED 800K revenue in 6 months), but investors won't know what market you're actually attacking without a dedicated slide showing TAM, SAM, and SOM for UAE, Saudi Arabia, and the UK. Add one slide that breaks down the addressable market by geography and customer segment (B2B developers vs. B2C buyers), then show where MIRA sits within that—for example, "UAE residential real estate commissions = $2.8B annually; MIRA targeting 5% of developer partnerships in year one = $140M opportunity."

findings.label: Does the deck include a Cap Table or Funding slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 4

findings.label: Does the deck include a Competition or Competitive Landscape slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as COMPETITION
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide mapping MIRA against 3-4 specific competitors in UAE/MENA (Property Finder, Bayut, Huspy, Dubizzle) with a simple comparison table showing how MIRA differs on key dimensions like AI automation level, commission structure, property visualization tech, and developer integrations—this directly addresses the due diligence report's repeated flag that competitive differentiation is unclear and will give investors confidence you understand your market position.

findings.label: Does the deck claim AI, ML, or proprietary technology? If so, does it provide any technical substance — architecture, methodology, or differentiation — beyond marketing language?
findings.evidence: LLM returned non-JSON after retry
findings.suggestion: Right now you're listing "AR/VR + NLP + CRM" as your tech stack, but investors will ask what's actually proprietary here—and you need a concrete answer before they do. Add one slide showing your specific AI architecture: for example, "Custom fine-tuned LLM trained on 50K+ UAE property transactions for price forecasting (±5% accuracy)" or "Proprietary emotional-intelligence layer that flags buyer intent signals, reducing agent follow-up time by 40%"—pick the one thing that's genuinely yours and defensible, then back it with a metric or technical detail that proves you've built it, not just assembled third-party tools.

findings.label: Does the deck explain any defensible moat — IP, patents, proprietary data, network effects, or switching costs?
findings.evidence: unclear if MIRA has strong defensible IP or first-mover advantage
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide on IP and defensibility that spells out exactly what you own: list any patents filed (even pending ones), proprietary datasets you've built from your pilot deals, exclusive partnerships with DLD or developers that competitors can't easily replicate, and the specific switching costs you've created—for example, "agents who integrate MIRA into their workflow lose access to 18 months of buyer preference data if they switch." Right now you're claiming "deep integration" but not explaining why a competitor couldn't build the same thing in 6 months.

findings.label: Does the deck describe the product's current development stage — idea, prototype, beta, live, or scaling?
findings.evidence: What is the current development status of MIRA's AI/AR platform? Is the technology live, beta, or roadmap?
findings.suggestion: Add a single, clear line to your technology slide stating exactly where MIRA stands: "Platform Status: Live in UAE with 15+ active developer partners, processing 200+ monthly transactions" (or whatever your actual numbers are). Right now you're claiming "strong traction and revenue" but investors can't tell if you're running a pilot with five properties or a functioning product at scale—nail down the stage so the 70% conversion rate and customer metrics actually land as proof points instead of raising more questions.

findings.label: Does the deck describe a clear and logical customer journey or product workflow?
findings.evidence: Can you provide demonstrations or live access to the full customer journey, showcasing all claimed functionalities?
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide that walks through a real customer interaction end-to-end—show a buyer logging in, getting matched to a property via AI, viewing it in AR, receiving an ROI forecast, and closing the deal—with actual screenshots or a 2-minute walkthrough video embedded. This turns your claimed 70% conversion rate and 90% satisfaction from abstract metrics into something investors can actually see working, and directly answers the "can you demonstrate the full customer journey" question that's blocking them.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any third-party technology dependencies that could represent a risk — cloud providers, APIs, open-source components?
findings.evidence: Is the AI model proprietary or built using third-party LLMs? Is there any IP protection?
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated section to your technology slide that explicitly states whether your AI model is proprietary or built on third-party LLMs (like OpenAI's GPT), and detail your IP protection strategy—for example: "MIRA's NLP engine is built on OpenAI's API for core language processing, but our proprietary layer includes custom fine-tuning on 50K+ UAE real estate transactions and a custom emotional intelligence classifier that we've filed a provisional patent for." This directly addresses the investor question already flagged on your slide and removes ambiguity around what's actually defensible versus commodity tech.

findings.label: Does the deck address data privacy, security, or compliance relevant to the product?
findings.evidence: How is data privacy and security ensured, especially for high net worth and institutional clients?
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated security & compliance section that names specific certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2, GDPR compliance status) and describes your data handling architecture for high-net-worth clients—for example: "Client financial data and property portfolios are encrypted end-to-end, stored in UAE-regulated data centers, with role-based access controls limiting agent visibility to assigned properties only." This directly addresses investor concerns about handling sensitive client information across multiple markets with different regulatory requirements.

findings.label: Do the financial projections include stated assumptions — growth rate basis, customer acquisition assumptions, or cost structure logic?

findings.label: Does the deck provide unit economics — CAC, LTV, LTV:CAC ratio, or payback period?
findings.evidence: Please provide CAC vs. LTV data per customer type (B2B and B2C).
findings.suggestion: Add a new slide showing your unit economics split by B2B (developer partners) and B2C (individual buyers): for B2B, show how much it costs to acquire a developer partner (sales time, integration costs) versus their lifetime value (AED800K revenue suggests some partners are already paying—calculate annual contract value × expected years); for B2C, show cost per buyer acquired through your current channels versus what each buyer spends over time. Even rough estimates backed by your actual customer data (like "we spent 2 months of founder time to land our first developer at AED150K annual contract" or "our B2C buyers cost us AED200 to acquire and spend AED1,

findings.label: Does the deck provide evidence of paying customers — not just users, pilots, or sign-ups?
findings.evidence: What proportion of claimed AED800K revenue is recurring, and what is derived from one-off deals?
findings.suggestion: Break down your AED800K revenue into two clear numbers: how much is recurring monthly/annual contracts with real estate developers or agents, and how much came from one-time deals or pilots. For example, if you have Emaar on a 12-month AED50K/month contract and three smaller one-off projects totaling AED200K, show exactly that—investors need to see the revenue that will actually repeat and grow your business.

findings.label: Does the deck describe a specific, credible go-to-market strategy — not just 'sales and marketing'?
findings.evidence: What is your go-to-market strategy in neCw geographies (e.g., expansion to Saudi Arabia, UK)? How do you plan to acquire customers in these markets – organic vs. paid strategies? How do you plan to compete with established digital brokerage platforms and portals? Is there an internal sales team oOr partner-led channel strategy?
findings.suggestion: Right now you're listing questions, not answering them — flip this to show your actual GTM playbook for Saudi Arabia and the UK with concrete channels. For example: "Saudi Arabia: Partner with Emaar and Dar Al-Baraka (signed LOI pending) to embed MIRA in their portals, targeting 500 qualified leads monthly at $8 CAC; UK: Launch paid search targeting expat investors searching 'buy Dubai property from UK' with 15% conversion to qualified leads at $45 CAC, competing on speed (MIRA responds in 2 minutes vs. 24-hour broker response)." Show the specific partners, unit economics, and competitive edge per

findings.label: Does the deck show month-over-month or year-over-year growth with specific numbers?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a simple growth chart to your traction slide showing your AED800K revenue claim with month-over-month progression—for example, "Month 1: AED50K → Month 6: AED180K → Month 12: AED800K" with the actual percentage growth rate called out (e.g., "156% MoM growth"). This gives investors concrete proof your traction is real and accelerating, not just a single number they have to take on faith.

findings.label: Does the deck mention customer retention, churn rate, or NPS?
findings.evidence: Please provide customer feedback, case studies, or NPS scores from early adopters.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any named customer logos, case studies, or testimonials?

This transforms your AED800K revenue claim from a number into proof that real, recognizable players in UAE real estate trust your product.

findings.label: Does the team have a gap in a critical function — for example no technical co-founder for a tech product, or no sales lead for a B2B company?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've got strong functional coverage across your advisory team, but investors will want to see who's actually running the company day-to-day—add a dedicated founder/CEO section above your advisors that clearly names your core founding team with their roles and 1-2 key credentials each (for example: "Sarah Chen, CEO & Co-founder, previously scaled operations at [company] from $2M to $50M ARR"). Right now it looks like you're all advisors with no clear leadership structure, which raises questions about who's accountable for execution.

findings.label: Does the deck provide relevant prior experience for each founder — domain expertise, prior startups, or industry background?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Right now investors see a list of advisors with their expertise areas, but nothing about the founding team's own backgrounds—who started this company, what they built before, or why they're uniquely qualified to run it. Add a dedicated section with 2-3 founding team members (names, photos, and a one-liner each) that connects their past wins to what they're building now—for example, "Founder A: Previously scaled two SaaS companies to $10M ARR in the Middle East, now leading Deal360's expansion into family offices" or "Founder B: 8 years in venture capital at [Fund Name], invested in 15+ exits, now building the infrastructure these founders need

findings.label: Does the deck mention any advisors, board members, or notable backers with relevant expertise?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've got strong individual experts listed, but investors need to see who's actually leading your company—add a dedicated "Leadership Team" section at the top of slide 10 with your CEO, co-founders, and key executives (name, title, and 1-2 lines on relevant experience), then keep your advisors below as a separate group. For example: "Ahmed Hassan, CEO & Founder — 12 years in UAE startup ecosystem, previously built two exits in fintech" followed by your advisor roster, so it's immediately clear who's steering the ship versus who's advising it.

findings.label: Does the deck mention founder vesting schedules or equity arrangements?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a simple one-liner to your Team slide stating the founder and key co-founder equity stakes—something like "Founder: 60% | Co-founder A: 25% | Co-founder B: 15%" with a note that all equity is on a standard 4-year vesting schedule with a 1-year cliff. Investors need to see this upfront to understand incentive alignment and avoid asking about it in every meeting, so get ahead of it now.

findings.label: Does the deck operate in a regulated industry — fintech, healthtech, edtech, legaltech — without mentioning relevant licences, compliance, or regulatory approvals?
findings.evidence: MIRA positions itself as an AI-powered real estate super agent that addresses these pain points by providing instant, 24/7 service. Leveraging smart algorithms, CRM integration, and real-time valuation tools, MIRA manages the entire client journey—from lead qualification to post-sale support.
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated compliance slide that maps MIRA's specific regulatory requirements in UAE, Saudi Arabia, and UK—such as real estate brokerage licenses, AI agent certifications, data protection standards (GDPR for UK, local equivalents for MENA), and any approvals already obtained from Dubai Land Department or local authorities. For example: "UAE Compliance: Real Estate Broker License (in progress, Q1 2025) | DLD API Integration (approved) | Data Privacy: ADISA-compliant encryption for investor records | UK Expansion: FCA consultation underway for AI-assisted advisory services." This shows investors you've thought through the regulatory landscape rather than treating it as an afterthought

findings.label: Does the deck mention any pending litigation, IP disputes, or legal encumbrances?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide addressing your legal and regulatory standing in the UAE real estate market—specifically: what real estate brokerage licenses or AI agent certifications you currently hold, any pending approvals from DFSA or DLD, and confirmation that your platform complies with UAE data protection laws (especially for handling foreign investor data). For example: "MIRA Regulatory Status: Licensed as [specific license type] by [regulator], compliant with ADISA data handling requirements, no pending disputes or regulatory investigations." This removes a major investor concern upfront rather than letting it surface during due diligence.

findings.label: Are there any numerical inconsistencies across slides — for example revenue figures that contradict each other, or growth rates that do not match the underlying numbers?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Good news—your numbers check out cleanly across the deck, so you don't need to chase down inconsistencies. What you *do* need to add is a simple waterfall or bridge showing how your AED 800K revenue breaks down by stream (commission share vs. developer partnerships vs. ancillary services vs. SaaS), because right now investors can't tell if that's mostly one-off deal commissions or if you've actually built recurring revenue. Add a single visual on your financials slide showing revenue by source for the past 6 months—this transforms a single impressive number into proof that your diversified model is actually working.

findings.label: Does the deck make any extraordinary claims — market leadership, unique technology, or exceptional growth — without supporting evidence?
findings.evidence: MIRA positions itself as an AI-powered real estate super agent that addresses these pain points by providing instant, 24/7 service... This automation and intelligence is intended to disrupt local and global real estate markets by delivering scalable, data-driven services that traditional agents cannot match

findings.label: Is the deck professionally presented — consistent formatting, no spelling errors, no placeholder text?
findings.evidence: TFFhaae uudggateeatt c TToneetccahhinnnsoo, hllooerggeyyin AA, inns nninuuteaanll dRReeedpp sooolrrettly
findings.suggestion: You've got garbled text scattered throughout your footer and disclaimer sections—that "TFFhaae uudggateeatt c TToneetccahhinnnsoo" string appears repeatedly and completely undermines your professionalism. Replace all instances of this corrupted text with a clean, single-line disclaimer like "This document is confidential and for authorized recipients only" so investors see a polished, finished deck instead of one that looks like it has encoding errors.
